
Another important quantity is the **hazard function**:

{{< include _subfiles/shared/_def-hazard.qmd >}}

---

{{< include def-incidence-rate.qmd >}}

---

::: notes
The hazard function has an important relationship to the density and survival functions,
which we can use to derive the hazard function for a given probability distribution (@thm-hazard-dens-surv).
:::

:::::{#lem-joint-prob-same-var}

#### Joint probability of a variable with itself

$$p(T=t, T\ge t) = p(T=t)$$

::::::{.proof}
By @thm-prob-subset, if $A\subseteq B$ then $\Pr(A \cap B) = \Pr(A)$.
In particular, $\{T=t\} \subseteq \{T\geq t\}$, so $p(T=t, T\ge t) = p(T=t)$.
::::::
:::::

---

:::{#thm-hazard-dens-surv}
#### Hazard equals density over survival
$$\haz(t)=\frac{f(t)}{\surv(t)}$$
:::

---

::::{.proof}

$$
\begin{aligned}
\haz(t) &=p(T=t|T\ge t)\\
&=\frac{p(T=t, T\ge t)}{p(T \ge t)}\\
&=\frac{p(T=t)}{p(T \ge t)}\\
&=\frac{f(t)}{\surv(t)}
\end{aligned}
$$

::::

---

:::{#exm-exp-haz}
##### exponential distribution

The hazard function of the exponential distribution family of models is:

$$
\ba
P(T=t|T \ge t)
&= \frac{f(t)}{\surv(t)}\\
&= \frac{\1{t \ge 0}\cdot \haz  \ef{-\haz t}}{\ef{-\haz t}}\\
&=\1{t \ge 0}\cdot \haz
\ea
$$
@fig-exp-hazard shows some examples of exponential hazard functions.

:::

---

```{r, echo = FALSE}
#| fig-cap: "Examples of hazard functions for exponential distributions"
#| label: fig-exp-hazard
library(ggplot2)
ggplot() +
  geom_hline(
    aes(col = "0.5", yintercept = 0.5)
  ) +
  geom_hline(
    aes(col = "1", yintercept = 1)
  ) +
  geom_hline(
    aes(col = "1.5", yintercept = 1.5)
  ) +
  geom_hline(
    aes(col = "5", yintercept = 5)
  ) +
  theme_bw() +
  ylab(expr(lambda)) +
  ylim(0, 5) +
  guides(col = guide_legend(title = expr(lambda))) +
  xlab("Time (t)") +
  xlim(0, 2.5) +
  theme(
    axis.title.x =
      element_text(
        angle = 0,
        vjust = 1,
        hjust = 1
      ),
    axis.title.y =
      element_text(
        angle = 0,
        vjust = 1,
        hjust = 1
      )
  )
```

---

We can also view the hazard function as the derivative of the negative of the logarithm of the survival function:

:::{#thm-h-logS}

#### transform survival to hazard

$$\haz(t) = \deriv{t}\cb{-\log{\surv(t)}}$$
:::

---

::::{.proof}
$$
\begin{aligned}
\haz(t)
&= \frac{f(t)}{\surv(t)}\\
&= \frac{-S'(t)}{\surv(t)}\\
&= -\frac{S'(t)}{\surv(t)}\\
&=-\deriv{t}\log{\surv(t)}\\
&=\deriv{t}\cb{-\log{\surv(t)}}
\end{aligned}
$$
::::

{{< slidebreak >}}

:::{#def-hazard-ratio}
#### hazard ratio
$$\defHR$$
:::

{{< slidebreak >}}

:::{#def-kappa}

#### Conditional survival probability at an event time

At event time $t_i$,
the **conditional survival probability**
is

$$\cs_i \eqdef \p(T > t_i \mid T \ge t_i).$$

:::

{{< slidebreak >}}

:::{#thm-kappa-complement}

#### Conditional survival is the complement of the discrete hazard increment

Let
$\haz_i \eqdef \p(T=t_i \mid T \ge t_i)$
be the discrete hazard increment
at event time $t_i$.
Then
$\cs_i = 1 - \haz_i$.

:::

::::{.proof}

$$
\ba
\cs_i
&= \p(T > t_i \mid T \ge t_i) \\
&= 1 - \p(T \le t_i \mid T \ge t_i) \\
&= 1 - \p(T = t_i \mid T \ge t_i) \\
&= 1 - \haz_i.
\ea
$$

::::
